topic: Someone finds out they have a half-sibling they never knew about
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope felt like a lead weight in my hand. Inside, a single, crisp photograph and a name that made my stomach clench. Liam. My mother’s handwriting. I stared at the picture—a grinning boy with eyes that were startlingly familiar—and a hot, tight pressure built behind my ribs. I ripped the paper in half, the sound sharp in the quiet apartment, and threw the pieces across the room. Every muscle in my jaw was clenched so tightly I could taste copper. How could she keep this from me? The sheer audacity of it made my hands tremble, and I paced the length of the living room, kicking at the rug as if trying to physically shake the revelation out of the air. I needed answers, and I needed them now.

[story 2]

I found the file tucked deep within my father’s old desk drawer. It was a brittle document, a birth certificate with a name I didn't recognize, and a picture of a child who looked eerily like me. A slow, building heat started in my chest, a furnace igniting beneath my sternum. I slammed the drawer shut so hard the wood shuddered. My breath hitched, shallow and ragged. I walked to the window, the glass cold against my forehead, and watched the city lights blur. My knuckles were white where I gripped the windowsill. This wasn't just information; it was a betrayal, a secret held over my head for years. I spun around, my voice coming out low and dangerously steady. "No. This isn't real."

[story 3]

The phone call ended, leaving a ringing silence that screamed in my ears. The words he whispered—'You have a brother, Sarah'—had detonated something inside me. I dropped the phone onto the hardwood floor; it didn't break, but the impact felt like a physical blow. I stood frozen for a moment, my vision tunneling. Then, the control snapped. I moved with sudden, violent purpose, grabbing the nearest heavy object—a ceramic vase—and hurled it against the wall. Shards of porcelain scattered across the plaster. My chest was heaving, each inhale a ragged gasp for air. I couldn't process the shock; I could only feel a white-hot surge of fury demanding release. I paced back and forth, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, my eyes darting around the room, searching for the source of the deception.
